sourceTitle: How engineering and product teams use Notion for sprint planning

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a sprint board in Notion?

options.text: To store all project documentation in one place
options.explanation: Documentation is stored in pages and databases, not specifically on the sprint board.

options.text: To view and manage tasks for the current sprint, grouped by project and status
options.explanation: The sprint board shows all tasks in the current sprint, organized by project and status, enabling focused work.

options.text: To automatically generate code from task descriptions
options.explanation: Notion does not generate code; it manages work items.

options.text: To replace GitHub pull request reviews entirely
options.explanation: GitHub integration complements Notion but does not replace PR reviews.

questions.question: Which step is NOT required when adding the sprint planning template to a workspace?

options.text: Open the in‑app Templates menu and add the Projects, tasks, and sprints template
options.explanation: This is the first required step.

options.text: Create a new workspace from scratch before adding the template
options.explanation: A new workspace is not needed; the template can be added to any existing workspace.

options.text: Add tasks and projects to the newly created databases
options.explanation: Populating the databases is a required step before planning sprints.

options.text: Navigate to the Sprint board to start managing tasks
options.explanation: This is the final step after the template is in place.

questions.question: How does Notion determine which tasks appear in the default Sprint board view?

options.text: It shows all tasks that have a due date within the next 30 days
options.explanation: The default view filters by sprint, not by generic due dates.

options.text: It filters to tasks that belong to the current sprint only
options.explanation: The default Sprint board view is set to display tasks assigned to the current sprint, removing distractions.

options.text: It displays tasks based on the assignee’s role (engineer vs. product)
options.explanation: Role‑based filtering is not part of the default view.

options.text: It randomly selects a subset of tasks each time the board loads
options.explanation: The view is deterministic, not random.

questions.question: When completing a sprint, what options does Notion give you for handling unfinished tasks?

options.text: Delete them permanently from the workspace
options.explanation: Notion never forces deletion of unfinished tasks during sprint completion.

options.text: Move them to the next sprint, send them to the backlog, or keep them in the current sprint
options.explanation: You can choose to move high‑priority tasks forward, send others to the backlog, or leave them where they are.

options.text: Archive them automatically after 24 hours
options.explanation: Archiving is not an automatic step in sprint completion.

options.text: Convert them into GitHub issues without user input
options.explanation: Conversion to GitHub issues requires manual linking, not automatic conversion.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Unique Task ID when linking Notion tasks with GitHub pull requests?

options.text: It serves as a password to protect the pull request
options.explanation: The ID is not a security credential; it’s an identifier.

options.text: It allows Notion to automatically display the PR status within the task record
options.explanation: The ID creates a cross‑reference so Notion can show PR status and update the task.

options.text: It replaces the need for a GitHub account
options.explanation: A GitHub account is still required to create pull requests.

options.text: It syncs the task’s description with the PR’s code diff
options.explanation: The ID only links the objects; it does not merge content.

questions.question: Which Notion feature enables you to create a personalized view that only shows tasks assigned to the person currently viewing the database?

options.text: A static filter set to a specific user’s name
options.explanation: A static filter would only ever show that one user’s tasks, not adapt per viewer.

options.text: A dynamic filter using the “Me” keyword on the Assignee property
options.explanation: Using “Me” makes the filter resolve to the current viewer, providing a personalized view.

options.text: A formula property that concatenates the viewer’s email with task titles
options.explanation: Formulas can display data but cannot dynamically filter the view based on the viewer.

options.text: A linked database that only imports tasks from a separate workspace
options.explanation: Linked databases replicate data but don’t provide per‑viewer filtering.

questions.question: How can Notion AI help a product team during sprint planning?

options.text: It automatically assigns tasks to the most senior engineer
options.explanation: Task assignment is a manual decision; AI does not auto‑assign based on seniority.

options.text: It can generate user stories, extract risks, and brainstorm ideas based on project page content
options.explanation: AI can write user stories, pull out blockers, and help brainstorm, accelerating planning.

options.text: It replaces the sprint board with a Kanban board automatically
options.explanation: AI does not change the board type; it provides content assistance.

options.text: It creates a new sprint every day without user input
options.explanation: Sprint creation still follows the defined cadence; AI does not auto‑generate daily sprints.

questions.question: What does the “Completed tasks” property on the Sprint board display?

options.text: A list of tasks that were moved to the backlog
options.explanation: Backlog tasks are incomplete, not counted as completed.

options.text: A progress ring showing the percentage of tasks finished in the sprint
options.explanation: The property visualizes sprint progress as a percentage ring.

options.text: The total number of hours logged on each task
options.explanation: Hours logged are not represented by this property.

options.text: The number of pull requests linked to the sprint
options.explanation: Pull request count is unrelated to the Completed tasks property.

questions.question: Which view would you use to identify tasks that have not yet been assigned to any sprint?

options.text: Sprint planning view
options.explanation: This view groups tasks by sprint, showing only those already assigned.

options.text: Backlog view
options.explanation: The Backlog view displays incomplete tasks without a sprint assignment.

options.text: Current sprint view
options.explanation: This view only shows tasks within the active sprint.

options.text: Project overview view
options.explanation: The project view aggregates projects, not unassigned tasks.

questions.question: What benefit does using a Relation property between projects and documentation provide?

options.text: It automatically publishes the documentation to the public web
options.explanation: Relation links do not change publishing settings.

options.text: It lets team members click through from a project to its relevant docs, keeping context close at hand
options.explanation: Relations create clickable links, making related docs easily accessible.

options.text: It merges the content of the documentation into the project page
options.explanation: Relations link, not merge, content.

options.text: It encrypts the documentation so only project owners can view it
options.explanation: Encryption is not a function of Relation properties.
